1: nôdāʿ bîhûdāh ʾĕlōhîm — God is known in Judah. The niphal of yādaʿ (to be known, to make oneself known) — the same root as the covenant-knowledge formula. bĕyisrāʾêl gādôl shĕmô — his name is great in Israel. The pairing of Judah and Israel is a merism for the whole people — the divine fame is national. The victory celebrated in vv3-6 is YHWH's public demonstration of this greatness; the hymn opens by naming the result before explaining the cause.
2: wayhî bĕshālēm sukkô — his tabernacle is in Salem. Shālēm (Salem) is the ancient name for Jerusalem (Gen 14:18; Heb 7:2) — its connection to shālôm (wholeness, peace) is lexically transparent. Sukkô (his booth/tabernacle — from sukkāh, the temporary dwelling, used of the Feast of Booths) — the warrior-God dwells in the city he defends. ûmĕʿônātô bĕṣiyyôn — his dwelling place is in Zion. Mĕʿônāh (dwelling place, den) — the permanent habitation that makes Salem/Zion the location of divine judgment.
3: shāmmāh shibbēr rishpê-qāshet — there he shattered the flashing arrows. Risphê qāshet (flames/sparks of the bow — fiery arrows) — the image combines fire and combat. Shibbar (piel of shābar: to shatter, smash to pieces) — the weapons of war broken at the source, in the presence of God at his sanctuary. māgēn wĕḥereb ûmilḥāmāh selāh — the shield, the sword, and all the weapons of war. The catalog (bow-arrows, shield, sword) covers the full range of ancient warfare — nothing escaped the divine judgment. There (shāmmāh, at Zion) is the location of total disarmament.
4: nāʾôr ʾattāh ʾaddîr mêharrê-ṭāref — you are glorious and majestic, more than the everlasting mountains of plunder. Ṭāref (prey, plunder, what a predator tears) — the mountains of plunder are either the lair-mountains of predators or the accumulated trophies of powerful nations. YHWH is more majestic than either. The comparison is deliberately jarring: the greatest thing one can imagine (prey-mountains, the pinnacle of military success) cannot approach the divine splendor.
5: ʾeshshôlĕlû ʾabbîrê lēb — the valiant warriors have been stripped of their plunder. ʾAbbîr lēb (mighty of heart — those of formidable courage) — the ironic reversal: those who came to plunder are themselves stripped (shālal). nāmû shĕnātām — they have fallen into the sleep of death. Nûm (to sleep, to slumber) as a euphemism for death — the mighty warriors permanently put to sleep. wĕlōʾ-māṣĕʾû khol-ʾanshê-ḥayil ʾet-yĕdêhem — not one soldier could lift a hand. Even the reflexive act of raising a hand in defense was beyond them — a total incapacity imposed by divine judgment.
6: mîgaʿarātĕkhā ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb — at your rebuke, O God of Jacob. Gaʿar (to rebuke, to thunder at — used of the divine rebuking of the sea in Ps 104:7; 106:9; Nah 1:4) — the divine rebuke is the audible power that shatters armies. The same rebuke that divided the sea at the Exodus here destroys the war machine. nirdāam wākkerekeb wāssûs — both chariot and horse fell into a dead sleep. Rādam (to be in a deep stupor, a death-like sleep — the same root as Jonah 1:5's nirdām) — the chariot force cast into helpless unconsciousness. The chariot was ancient warfare's decisive weapon; its defeat signals total military reversal.
7: ʾattāh nôrāʾ ʾattāh — but you — you alone are to be feared. The pronoun ʾattāh (you) appears twice for emphasis — an insistent identification of who, exactly, inspires fear. Nôrāʾ (fearful, awesome — niphal participle of yārēʾ) — applied to God at Sinai (Deut 10:17) and to the angel of YHWH. ûmî-yaʿămōd lĕphānêkhā mēʾāz ʾappekā — who can stand before you when your anger is kindled? The rhetorical question expects no answer — the defeated armies of vv5-6 are the empirical demonstration. The question frames divine wrath not as capricious but as a self-evident power before which nothing can stand.
8: mishāmayim hishmaʿtā mišpāṭ — from heaven you pronounced judgment. Hishmaʿtā (hiphil of shāmaʿ: you caused to be heard, you announced) — judgment is audible, announced from the heavens and heard on earth. ʾāreṣ yārĕʾāh wĕshāqāṭāh — the earth feared and stood still. Earth personified as a witness to the divine verdict: it heard and froze in awe. The earth's response mirrors the proper human response that the invaders refused to give — they came with horns raised (Ps 75:4-5); the earth itself bowed.
9: bĕqûm-lammišpāṭ ʾĕlōhîm — when God arose to judge. The divine arising (qûm) is the standard idiom for God moving from apparent inactivity into decisive intervention (cf. Ps 7:6; 68:1; 74:22). lĕhôshîaʿ kol-ʿanwê-ʾāreṣ selāh — to save all the humble of the earth. The ʿānāw (the humble, the meek, the lowly — those who have bent their necks in contrast to the stiff-necked of Ps 75:5) — those with no human recourse. The selah marks the theological center: God's judgment serves his saving purpose for the vulnerable.
10: kî-ḥămat ʾādām tôdekhā — surely human fury will bring you praise. Ḥēmāh (fury, burning anger — the hot rage of the enemy) becomes an occasion for YHWH's praise — the paradox of divine sovereignty transforming human wrath into divine glorification. shĕʾērît ḥēmōt taḥgōr — whatever wrath remains you will restrain. Tāḥgōr (you will gird on, restrain — from ḥāgar: to gird about, as a warrior girds on a weapon) — God wearing the remaining human wrath as a controlled girdle. Both the wrath unleashed and the wrath restrained serve the divine purpose.
11: nidrû wĕshallĕmû laYHWH ʾĕlōhêkhem — make vows to the LORD your God and keep them. The victory hymn turns to exhortation: the proper response to witnessed divine power is vow-making and vow-keeping. kol-sĕbîbāyw yōbîlû shay lammôrāʾ — let all the surrounding nations bring tribute to the one who is to be feared. Shay (tribute, gift — a rare term, cf. Isa 18:7; Ps 68:29) — the surrounding nations, seeing the invader's defeat, are called to bring tribute to the mōrāʾ (the one who inspires fear). The national humiliation of the aggressor is the universal invitation.
12: yibṣōr rûaḥ nĕgîdîm — he cuts off the spirit of princes. Bāṣar (to cut off, to harvest — used of cutting grapes) — the breath/spirit (rûaḥ) of princes snipped like a cluster. Nāgîd (prince, leader — one set before others) — those in positions of earthly authority, whose authority and breath are equally in God's hands. nôrāʾ lĕmalkê-ʾāreṣ — he is fearsome to the kings of the earth. The psalm closes with nôrāʾ (fearsome) — the same word centered in v7. Kings feared by their subjects are themselves made to fear the God who cuts off the breath of princes.
